# **CS 584 Natural Language Processing** 

## **Introduction** 

Ping Wang Department of Computer Science Stevens Institute of Technology

## **Course Information** 

- :

3

## **What do we learn from this course?** 

1. Understanding of basic techniques of _processing_ human languages and the difficulties in _understanding_ languages. 

2. Understanding of effective modern methods for NLP • recurrent neural networks, attention, convolutional neural networks, transformers, LLMs, etc 

3. Understanding of and getting the ability to build systems for some major tasks in NLP 

   - word semantics, dependency parsing, document classification, language modeling, machine translation, language generation, etc. 

7

## **Course Outcomes** 

1. Implement gradient descent (GD) and stochastic gradient descent (SGD) techniques for learning problems and understand the theory behind them. 

2. Apply word2vec models in real-world text corpora. 

3. Understand the neural networks models and backpropagation optimization. Implement neural network models in TensorFlow or others. 

4. Understand dependency parsing, recurrent neural networks and their application in NLP. 

5. Understand convolutional neural networks and their application in NLP. 

6. Understand sequence to sequence models and attention in NLP deep neural networks. 

7. Understand the recent advances in NLP, such as transformers, pretraining, fine-tuning, prompting, etc. 

8

## **Assignments** 

- **Goal:** test your ability to understand knowledge of NLP 

- Mix of written and programming problems. 

- Submission: Upload your e-copies (PDF file and codes files) on Canvas 

- Jupyter Notebook is recommended to use for your programming assignments (with code and output after execution). Make sure to include detailed comments and analysis in your code. 

11

- Team: should be a group project with two students 

- Requirements 

- More details will be provided in the project guidelines. 

12

## **Grading** 

## Distribution of (0-100):

[Visual Description]: This slide presents a detailed letter grade distribution scale used for academic assessment at Stevens Institute of Technology. The grading system converts numerical scores (0-100) into letter grades with plus/minus modifiers. The scale is as follows: A (90-100) represents excellent performance; A- (85-90) is near-excellent; B+ (80-85) is above average; B (75-80) is good/average performance; B- (70-75) is slightly below average but passing; C+ (65-70) and C (60-65) represent marginal passing grades; and F (<60) indicates failing performance. This type of grading scale is commonly used in higher education institutions in the United States to standardize student performance evaluation. The 10-point scale with 5-point intervals for plus/minus distinctions allows for more granular assessment than a simple A-F system without modifiers.

- Any plagiarism or other form of cheating will be dealt with under relevant Stevens policies.

## **Acknowledgements** 

Slides adapted from Dr. Christopher Manning’s Natural Language Processing with Deep Learning at Stanford. 

23

[Visual Description]: This slide is an acknowledgements slide from a presentation, indicating that the slides were adapted from Dr. Christopher Manning's 'Natural Language Processing with Deep Learning' course at Stanford University. The slide features the Stevens Institute of Technology branding, including their shield logo in the upper right corner and their name at the bottom. The slide number 23 is visible in the bottom right corner. This type of slide is commonly used in academic presentations to give credit to original sources and materials that have been reused or modified. Dr. Christopher Manning is a prominent figure in the field of natural language processing and deep learning, known for his influential research and teaching at Stanford University.

## **What is NLP?** 

Wikipedia: Natural language processing (NLP) is a subfield of **computer science** , **information engineering** , and **artificial intelligence** concerned with the **interactions** between computers and human (natural) languages, in particular how to program computers to process and analyze large amounts of natural language data. 

25

## **Go beyond the keyword matching** 

- ●Identify the structure and meaning of _words, sentences, texts and conversations_ 

- Deep understanding of broad language 

- ●NLP is all around us 

26

[Visual Description]: This educational slide from Stevens Institute of Technology illustrates the fundamental limitation of simple keyword matching in natural language processing and the necessity of deeper linguistic understanding. The central visual element is a two-panel cartoon by Gary Larson (The Far Side) showing the communication gap between humans and dogs. In the left panel ('What we say to dogs'), a man delivers a complex command to his dog Ginger: 'Okay, Ginger! I've had it! You stay out of the garbage! Understand, Ginger? Stay out of the garbage, or else!' In the right panel ('What they hear'), the same speech is rendered as 'Blah blah GINGER blah blah blah...' with only the dog's name being processed. This cartoon metaphorically demonstrates how keyword-only systems capture surface-level tokens while missing semantic content, structure, and intent. The slide emphasizes three core learning objectives: (1) identifying the structure and meaning at multiple linguistic levels—words, sentences, texts, and conversations; (2) achieving deep understanding of broad language rather than superficial pattern matching; and (3) recognizing that NLP technologies are ubiquitous in modern applications. The pedagogical purpose is to contrast shallow keyword matching with sophisticated NLP approaches that employ syntactic parsing, semantic analysis, pragmatics, and discourse understanding to comprehend language as humans do.

## **Text Classification** 

- Binary classification (e.g., spam or not, positive or negative) 

- Multi-class classification (e.g., based on themes or authorships) 

- Large Scale Hierarchical Text Classification (Kaggle ~~a~~ competition) 

32

[Visual Description]: This educational slide presents three fundamental types of text classification in natural language processing. First, binary classification involves categorizing text into one of two mutually exclusive classes, illustrated with a spam detection example where emails are classified as either 'spam' or 'not spam' (inbox), with a visual diagram showing emails entering a classifier and being sorted into two folders. Second, multi-class classification assigns text to one of multiple possible categories, demonstrated using The New York Times website navigation menu showing thematic sections including World, U.S., Politics, N.Y., Business, Opinion, Tech, Science, Health, Sports, Arts, Books, Style, Food, Travel, and Magazine—representing how articles are categorized by topic or authorship. Third, large-scale hierarchical text classification is introduced through reference to the LSHTC (Large Scale Hierarchical Text Classification) Challenge, a Kaggle competition, accompanied by a tree diagram showing multiple levels of classification nodes branching from root to leaf categories, indicating how classes are organized in parent-child relationships allowing for more granular and structured categorization schemes. The slide emphasizes the progression from simple two-class problems to complex, many-class hierarchical structures in text classification applications.

## **Question Answering**

- AILA: A Question Answering System in the Legal Domain. Huang et  al. IJCAI 2020.

## **Natural Language Instruction** 

Use **natural language** to provide instructions or guidance to machines, computers, robots, or other automated systems. Instead of **using formal programming languages or complex commands** , NLI allows users to communicate with technology in a more intuitive and human-friendly manner. 

- Grounding Natural Language Instructions to Mobile UI Actions. Yang Li, Google ~~_~~ Research et al. ACL 2020. [paper]. 

- — Translating Navigation Instructions in Natural Language to a High-Level Plan for Behavioral Robot —— Navigation. Zang et al. EMNLP 2018. 

- Inferring Maps and Behaviors — from Natural Language Instructions. Duvallet et al. ISER 2014. 

35

## **Digital personal assistant** 

More on natural language instruction 

## **Challenges:** 

Understanding the context of the instruction, dealing with ambiguities in language, and ensuring accurate interpretation 

## **Potential:** 

Making technology more accessible and user-friendly, reducing the barrier for non-technical users to interact with and control sophisticated systems. 

36

## **Information Extraction** 

## ❖ Unstructured text to database entries 

**Related tasks:** Tokenization, Part of Speech Tagging, Ontology Identification, Named Entity Recognition, Co-reference resolution, Syntax/Semantic Parsing, Semantic Role Labeling, Word  Embedding, Concept Discovery. 

37

[Visual Description]: This slide illustrates the concept of Information Extraction (IE), a natural language processing task that transforms unstructured text into structured database entries. The example shows a news article about executive appointments at the New York Times, with color-coded entities: yellow for organizations (New York Times Co., New York Times newspaper, the parent), red for person names (Russell T. Lewis, Lance R. Primis), and green for job titles/roles (president and general manager, executive vice president and deputy general manager, president and chief operating officer). Below the text, a structured table extracts three key facts: (1) Russell T. Lewis started as president and general manager of New York Times newspaper, (2) Russell T. Lewis ended his role as executive vice president at New York Times newspaper, and (3) Lance R. Primis started as president and CEO of New York Times Co. The slide also lists related NLP tasks essential for information extraction: Tokenization (breaking text into words), Part of Speech Tagging (identifying grammatical categories), Ontology Identification (recognizing domain concepts), Named Entity Recognition (identifying proper nouns like people, organizations, locations), Co-reference resolution (determining when different phrases refer to the same entity, such as 'He' referring to Russell T. Lewis), Syntax/Semantic Parsing (analyzing grammatical structure and meaning), Semantic Role Labeling (identifying who did what to whom), Word Embedding (numerical vector representations of words), and Concept Discovery (identifying abstract ideas in text).

## **Language Comprehension** 

Beyonce Giselle Knowles-Carter (born September 4, 1981) is an American singer, songwriter, record producer and actress. Born and raised in Houston, Texas, she performed in various singing and dancing [| competitions as a child, and rose to fame in the late 1990s as lead singer of R&B girl-group Destiny’s Child. Managed by her father, Mathew Knowles, the group became one of the world’s best-selling girl groups of all time. Their hiatus saw the release of Beyonce’s debut album, Dangerously in Love (2003), which [| established her as a solo artist worldwide, earned five Grammy Awards and featured the Billboard Hot 100 number-one singles “Crazy in Love” and “Baby Boy”. 

- ❖ Q: In what city and state did Beyonce grow up? 

- ❖ A: Houston, Texas 

- ❖ Q: What areas did Beyonce compete in when she was growing up? ❖ A: Singing and dancing 

- ❖ Q: When did Beyonce release Dangerously in love? ❖ A: 2003 

A (wikipedia) passage from the SQuAD 2.0 dataset with 3 sample questions and the labeled answer spans. [Rajpurkar et al. 2018] 

38

## **Ethics, Bias, and Interpretability in NLP** 

For example, gender bias is one of the most prominent issues in NLP. Some researchers discovered that many advanced word-embedding language models, such as GPT-3, tend to connect males with occupations that require higher level of education. 

- When the model is asked “what is the gender of a doctor?”, it would have much higher possibility to respond, “It’s male.” 

- In contrast, if the model is asked “what is the gender of a nurse?”, it would be more likely to respond, “It’s female.” 

- Furthermore, other researchers realized that when they translated “He is a nurse. She is a doctor.” to Hungarian and then translated it back to English, the sentence became “She is a nurse. He is a doctor.” 

This evidence clearly demonstrates how gender bias has truly appeared in NLP systems. 

Source link 

39

## **Collaborative language modeling** 

Instead of generate only the final texts, the collaborative language modeling allows to modify and refine the generated texts by multiple agents to obtain more accurate and high-quality final texts. 

PEER: A Collaborative Language Model. Schick et al. 2022. Link to Twitter [GIF] 

ChatGPT: Optimizing Language Models for Dialogue. OpenAI. Nov. 2022.

Large Language Model based Multi-Agents: A Survey of Progress and Challenges

[Visual Description]: This diagram illustrates the rising trend in LLM-based Multi-Agents research from March 2023 to December 2023, structured as a tree with five main branches emerging from a central root labeled 'LLM-Based Multi-Agents.' The vertical axis represents time in 3-month intervals (2023-03, 2023-06, 2023-09, 2023-12). The five main branches are: (1) Framework (orange/brown) - showing foundational systems like Camel, MetaGPT, AutoGen, and Agents with paper counts at leaf nodes; (2) Problem Solving (green) - with subcategories including Science Debate (yellow), Science Experiments (gray), Embodied Agents (green), and Software Development (blue), showing paper counts ranging from 1-6; (3) World Simulation (pink/purple) - with subcategories Society (blue), Psychology (gray), Recommender System (red), and Disease Propagation (black), showing substantial growth with counts up to 11 papers; (4) Agents Capabilities Acquisition (blue) - featuring DyLAN, ProAgent, LTC, and Self-Adaptive; and (5) Datasets and Benchmarks (teal) - including MagIC and SOTOPIA. The diagram uses a color-coded legend to distinguish subcategories, with numbers at leaf nodes indicating paper counts within each category at specific time intervals. The visualization demonstrates rapid growth in this research field, particularly in World Simulation and Problem Solving applications, with the Framework branch showing early foundational work and subsequent branches showing diversification into applications and evaluation methods.

## **Challenges in NLP** 

# ❖ **Complexity** in representing, learning, and using linguistic/situational/world/visual knowledge 

- ❖ Human languages are **ambiguous** (unlike programming and other formal languages) 

- ❖ Human language interpretation depends on real world, **common sense** , and **contextual** knowledge. 

46

## **Challenges - Ambiguity** 

- ❖ Word sense ambiguity: semantic ambiguity, when a word has more than one meanings. 

   - _`John killed the wolf`_ 

   - _`Bill killed the project`_ 

   - _`Mary killed Jane (at tennis or murdered her?)`_ 

47

[Visual Description]: This slide illustrates the concept of word sense ambiguity, a type of semantic ambiguity in natural language processing where a single word has multiple meanings depending on context. The slide presents three examples using the verb 'killed': (1) 'John killed the wolf' - where 'killed' means causing physical death to an animal; (2) 'Bill killed the project' - where 'killed' is metaphorical, meaning to terminate or cancel something; and (3) 'Mary killed Jane' - which is genuinely ambiguous between 'defeated her at tennis/sports' (figurative) versus 'murdered her' (literal physical death). The accompanying cartoon demonstrates referential ambiguity rather than word sense ambiguity: two penguins discuss a lost wife, but when one asks 'She died?', the other cannot identify which penguin is the wife among a group of identical-looking penguins, leading to the punchline about needing nametags. This visual reinforces how ambiguity in language creates comprehension challenges for both humans and computational systems.

## **Challenges - Ambiguity** 

## ❖ Word sense/meaning ambiguity 

- Get you an ambulance 

- or tell you you are an ambulance 

48

[Visual Description]: Visual could not be parsed.

## **Challenges - Ambiguity** 

## ❖ Prepositional Phrase (PP) attachment ambiguity 

Is the prepositional phrase attached high, modifying the verb "kill"? 

Or is it attached low, to the object NP "man"? 

49

[Visual Description]: Visual could not be parsed.

## **Challenges - Ambiguity** 

Headlines Don't Always Tell the Story Ambiguous headlines: 

- ❖ Include your children when baking cookies 

   - (in the activity of baking? or inside the cookies?) 

- ❖ Hospitals are Sued by 7 Foot Doctors (7 foot tall? Or 7 doctors?) 

- ❖ Eye drops off shelf (eye drops are taken off the shelf or an eye drops off a shelf) 

- ❖ Teacher Strikes Idle Kids (go on strikes? Or hit idle kids?) 

50

## **Challenges - Ambiguity** 

## Pronoun reference ambiguity 

51

[Visual Description]: This slide illustrates the concept of pronoun reference ambiguity, a common challenge in natural language processing and computational linguistics. The example shows a sentence with two potential antecedents: 'Dr. Macklin' (a human doctor in a white coat) and 'Champion' (his dachshund dog). The pronoun 'He' in the second sentence ('He just loves to give big, wet, sloppy kisses!') creates ambiguity because it could grammatically refer to either entity. However, world knowledge and semantic context suggest 'He' refers to the dog Champion, since dogs are known for giving 'big, wet, sloppy kisses,' whereas this would be unprofessional behavior for a physician. This example demonstrates how resolving pronoun ambiguity requires both syntactic analysis (determining what nouns could grammatically match the pronoun) and semantic/pragmatic reasoning (using real-world knowledge to select the most plausible interpretation). The image serves as a humorous, memorable case study for why coreference resolution is difficult for both humans and AI systems.

## **Challenges - Language is dynamic** 

Language evolves and changes, e.g. cyber lingo 

|LOL|Laugh out loud|
|---|---|
|G2G|Got to go|
|BFN|Bye for now|
|B4N|Bye for now|
|Idk|I don’t know|
|FWIW|For what it’s worth|
|LUWAMH|Love you with all my heart|



52

[Visual Description]: Visual could not be parsed.

## **Challenges - Scale** 

Bible (King James version) ~700K words Penn Tree bank ~4.5M words from wall street journal Newswire collection ~500M+ words Wikipedia ~3 billion words Web several billions of words 

53

[Visual Description]: This slide illustrates the concept of scale challenges in natural language processing and computational linguistics by comparing the sizes of various text corpora. The table presents five data sources in ascending order of size: (1) The Bible (King James version) contains approximately 700,000 words, representing a relatively small, well-curated text; (2) The Penn Treebank, a foundational linguistic resource, contains approximately 4.5 million words from the Wall Street Journal and is notable for being one of the first large-scale annotated corpora with syntactic parse trees; (3) Newswire collections contain 500 million or more words, representing a significant jump in scale; (4) Wikipedia contains approximately 3 billion words, demonstrating the massive scale of modern digital encyclopedias; and (5) The Web contains several billions of words, representing the largest and most unbounded text source. The progression from thousands to billions of words highlights how NLP systems must handle increasingly massive datasets, creating computational, storage, and algorithmic challenges. The Penn Treebank is hyperlinked, indicating its importance as a benchmark dataset. The slide is from Stevens Institute of Technology (slide 53), suggesting this is part of a lecture on computational linguistics, natural language processing, or data science.

## **Tokenization in NLP** 

## Tokenization is cutting input data into parts (symbols) that can be mapped (embedded) into a vector space. 

https://medium.com/@lmpo/tokenization-and-word-embeddings-the-building-blocks-of-advanced-nlp-c203b78bfd07 

55

[Visual Description]: Visual could not be parsed.

## **Learning Representations** 

56

[Visual Description]: This slide illustrates the concept of learning distributed vector representations (word embeddings) in natural language processing, specifically demonstrating how semantic and syntactic relationships are captured as geometric patterns in high-dimensional space. The left diagram shows 'Semantic Relationship' where the vector from 'Woman' to 'Man' is approximately parallel to the vector from 'Queen' to 'King', demonstrating that the embedding space captures gender relationships consistently. This famous example shows that vector arithmetic can capture analogies: King - Man + Woman ≈ Queen. The right diagram shows 'Syntactic Relationship' where the vector from 'Big' to 'Biggest' is parallel to the vector from 'Small' to 'Smallest', illustrating that grammatical patterns (superlative formation) are also encoded as consistent directional shifts in the embedding space. These visualizations demonstrate that well-trained word embeddings organize concepts so that relationships between words become linear translations in the vector space, enabling both semantic reasoning (analogies based on meaning) and syntactic reasoning (analogies based on grammatical structure). The key insight is that neural network-based embeddings like Word2Vec, GloVe, or FastText learn to place similar words nearby and preserve relational structure through vector geometry.

## **Part of speech tagging** 

## **Common closed-classes:** 

Assigning grammatical parts of speech (e.g., noun, verb, adjective) to each word in a sentence. 

- prepositions: on, under, over, near, by, at, from, to, with 

- particles: up, down, on, off, in, out, at, by 

- determiners: a, an, the 

- conjunctions: and, but, or, as, if, when 

- pronouns: she, who, I, others 

- auxiliary verbs: can, may, should, are 

- numerals: one, two, three, first, second, third 

## **Common open-classes:** 

- adjectives 

- adverbs 

- nouns 

- verbs (except auxiliary verbs) 

- interjections 

Chapter 8: https://web.stanford.edu/~jurafsky/slp3/8.pdf 

57

## **Python Review** 

## Why Python? 

# • Python is a widely used, general purpose programming language. 

- Easy to start working with. 

- Scientific computation functionality similar to Matlab and Octave. 

- Used by major deep learning frameworks such as PyTorch and TensorFlow. 

63

[Visual Description]: Visual could not be parsed.

## **Language basics** 

Python is a strongly-typed and dynamically-typed language. 

- ●Strongly-typed: Interpreter always “respects” the types of each variable.[1] 

- ●Dynamically-typed: “A variable is simply a value bound to a name.” [1] 

- ●Execution: Python is first interpreted into bytecode (.pyc) and then compiled by a VM implementation into machine instructions. (Most commonly using C.) 

   - ○Python is “slower”, but it can run highly optimized C/C++ subroutines which make scientific computing (e.g. matrix multiplication) really fast. 

https://wiki.python.org/moin/Why%20is%20Python%20a%20dynamic%20language%20and%20also%20a%20strongly%20typed%20language 

64

## **Language basics** 

- ●Strongly-typed: **`1+’1’ -> Error`** 

- ●Dynamically-typed: **`foo = [1,2,3]`** and later, **`foo = “hello”`** 

●Execution: **`np.dot(x, W) + b`** -> fast! 

65

[Visual Description]: This slide presents three fundamental characteristics of a programming language (likely Python, based on the syntax and NumPy reference). First, it demonstrates strong typing with the example `1 + '1'` producing an Error, meaning the language does not automatically convert between incompatible types (integers and strings) during operations. Second, it illustrates dynamic typing by showing that a variable `foo` can first be assigned a list `[1,2,3]` and later reassigned to a string `"hello"` without explicit type declarations, allowing variables to hold different types at different times during execution. Third, it highlights execution performance with the NumPy operation `np.dot(x, W) + b`, indicating that numerical computations using optimized libraries run fast, which is particularly important for machine learning and scientific computing applications where matrix operations are common.

## **Python Review - Common operations** 

**`x = 10`**

**`y = 3`** 

**`# Comments starts with the hash symbol`** 

**`x + y >> 13`** 

**`# addition`** 

**`x - y >> 7`** 

**`# subtraction`** 

**`x ** y >> 1000`** 

**`# exponentiation`** 

**`x/y >> 3`** 

**`# dividing two integers`** 

**`x / float(y) >> 3.333..`** 

- **`# type casting for float division`** 

**`str(x) +”+” + str(y) >>”10+3” # casting and string concatenation`** Example code from Andrej Karpathy’s tutorial: http://cs231n.github.io/python-numpy-tutorial/

## **Build-in values**

**`# usual boolean values # represents the null`** 

**`x = None`** 

**`# variables can be None`** 

**`array=[1,2,None]`** 

**`# lists can contain None`** 

**`def func():`** 

**`return None`** 

**`# functions can return None`** 

**`if [1,2]!=[3,4]:`** 

**`# can check for equality`** 

**`print ‘error!’`** 

67

## **List** 

**`names = [‘zach’, ‘emily’] names[0] == ‘zach’ names.append(‘alex’) len(names) == 3 print(names) >> [‘zach’,’emily’,’alex’] names.extend([‘aba’,’kevin’]) print(names) >> [‘zach’,’emily’,’alex’,‘aba’,’kevin’] names = [] # creates an empty list names = list() # also creates an empty list stuff=[1,[‘hello’,’bye’], 1.3, None] # can mix types`** 

68

## **List slicing** 

List elements can be accessed in convenient ways basic format: mylist[start_index:end_index] 

**`numbers = [0,1,2,3,4,5,6] numbers[0:3] == numbers[:3] >> [0,1,2] numbers[5:] == numbers[5:7] >>[5,6]`** 

**`numbers[-1] == 6 #negative index wraps around number[-3:] == [4,5,6] numbers[3:-2] == [3,4] # can mix and match`** 

69

## **Tuple** 

Tuple are immutable arrays 

**`names = (‘zach’, ‘emily’)`** 

**`names[0] == ‘zach’ len(names) == 2`** 

**`print(names) >> (‘zach’, ‘emily’) names[0] = ‘richard’`** 

**`>>typeerror:  'tuple' object does not support item assignment`** 

**`empty = tuple() # empty tuple`** 

**`single = (10,) # single-element tuple, comma matters!`** 

70

## **Dictionary** 

Dictionaries are hash maps 

**`phonebook = dict()`** 

**`#empty dictionary`** 

**`phonebook = {‘zach’: ‘12-68’} phonebook[‘emily’] = ‘35-65’ print(‘zach’ in phonebook) print(‘john’ in phonebook) del phonebook[‘zach’] print(phonebook)`** 

**`# dictionary with one item`** 

**`#add another item`** 

**`>> True`** 

**`>>False`** 

**`#delete an item`** 

**`>> {‘emily’:’36-65’}`** 

**`for name, number in phonebook.iteritems():`** 

**`print name, number`** 

**`>> emily 35-66`** 

71

## **Classes** 

## **`class Animal(object):`** 

**`def __init__(self, species, age): # Constructor `a = Animal(‘bird’, 10)` self.species = species # Refer to instance with `self` self.age = age # All instance variables are public`** 

**`def isPerson(self):`** 

**`return self.species == “Homo Sapiens” def ageOneYear(self):`** 

**`self.age += 1 class Dog(Animal): def ageOneYear(self): self.age += 7`** 

**`# Inherits Animal’s methods # Override for dog years`** 

72

## **Numpy - ndarray operations** 

**`import numpy as np z=np.array([[6,7],[8,9]]) print(np.max(z, axis=1)) >> [7,9]`** 

**`print(np.max(z, axis=1, keepdims=True))  >> [[7][9]] np.dot(x, W) or x.dot(W)` Note: shapes (N,) !=(1,N)** 

**`print(np.array([1,2,3]).T) >> [1  2  3]`** 

**`np.sum(np.array([1,2,3]), axis = 1) >> Error: axis 1 is out of bounds for array of dimension 1`** 

73

## **Indexing** 

**`x = np.random.random((3, 4))  # Random (3,4) matrix x[:]`** 

**`# Selects everything in x # Selects the 0th and 2nd rows`** 

**`x[np.array([0, 2]), :] x[1, 1:3]`** 

**`# Selects 1st row as 1-D vector # and 1st through 2nd elements # Boolean indexing`** 

**`x[x > 0.5]`** 

74

## **Broadcasting** 

**`x = np.random.random((3, 4)) # Random (3, 4) matrix y = np.random.random((3, 1)) # Random (3, 1) matrix z = np.random.random((1, 4)) # Random (1,4) vector x + y # Adds y to each column of x`** 

**`x * z`** 

**`# Multiplies z element-wise with each row of x`** 

**`print((y + y.T).shape) # Can give unexpected results!`** 

75

## **Efficiency** 

## **For loops will dramatically slow down your code (~10-100x)** 

**`for i in range(x.shape[0]):`** 

**`for j in range(x.shape[1]): x[i,j] **= 2`** 

**`x **=2 for i in range(100, 1000): for j in range(x.shape[1]): x[i, j] += 5 x[np.arange(100,1000), :] += 5`** 

76

[Visual Description]: This slide illustrates the critical performance difference between using explicit Python for loops versus vectorized NumPy operations when working with arrays. The educational concept demonstrates that for loops can slow down code by approximately 10-100 times compared to vectorized operations. The slide presents two examples: First, a nested for loop iterating through all elements of a 2D array x to square each element (x[i,j] **= 2) is shown in red as the inefficient approach, while the green alternative x **= 2 performs the same operation using NumPy's broadcasting. Second, another nested for loop adding 5 to elements in rows 100-999 (x[i, j] += 5) is shown in red, contrasted with the green vectorized alternative using NumPy indexing: x[np.arange(100,1000), :] += 5. The key takeaway is that NumPy's vectorized operations leverage optimized C implementations and avoid Python interpreter overhead, making them dramatically faster for numerical computations on arrays.

## **List comprehension** 

- Similar to map() from functional programming languages. 

- Can improve readability & make the code succinct. 

- Format: **`[func(x) for x in some_list]`** 

- Following are equivalent: 

**`squares = []`** 

**`for i in range(10):`** 

**`squares.append(i**2)`** 

**`squares = [i**2 for i in range(10)]`** 

- Can be conditional: 

**`odds = [i**2 for i in range(10) if i%2 == 1]`** 

77

## **Convenient syntax** 

- Multiple assignment / unpacking iterables 

**`x, y, z = [‘Tensorflow’, ‘PyTorch’, ‘Chainer’]`** 

**`age, name, pets = 20, ‘Joy’, [‘cat’]`** 

- Returning multiple items from a function 

**`def some_func():`** 

**`return 10, 1`** 

**`ten, one = some_func()`** 

- Joining list of strings with a delimiter 

**`“, ”.join([‘1’, ‘2’, ‘3’]) == ‘1, 2, 3’`** 

- String literals with both single and double quotes 

**`message = ‘I like “single” quotes.’ reply = “I prefer ‘double’ quotes.”`** 

78

## **Debugging tips** 

- Python has an interactive shell where you can execute arbitrary code 

   - Great replacement for TI-84 (no integer overflow!) 

   - Confused by syntax? Just try it in the shell! 

## **`$ python`** 

**`Python 2.7.10 (default, Jul 15 2017, 17:16:57)`** 

**`>>> 2 ** 5 / 2`** 

**`16`** 

**`>>> 2 ** (5 / 2)`** 

**`4`** 

- Can import any module (even custom ones in the current directory) 

- Try small test cases in the shell 

79

## **Debugging tips** 

## Unsure of what you can do with an object? **Use type() and dir()!!** 

**`>>> class Duck(object):`** 

**`...     def quack(self): pass`** 

**`...`** 

**`>>> bird = Duck()`** 

**`>>> type(bird)`** 

**`<class '__main__.Duck'>`** 

**`>>> dir(bird)`** 

**`['__class__', '__delattr__', '__dict__', '__doc__', '__format__', '__getattribute__', '__hash__', '__init__', '__module__', '__new__', '__reduce__', '__reduce_ex__', '__repr__', '__setattr__', '__sizeof__',`** 

**`'__str__', '__subclasshook__', '__weakref__', 'quack'] >>>`** 

80
topic: Unsure of what you can do with an object? Use type() and dir()!!

text:
## **Numpy debugging** 

- ●Print shapes to see if they match what you expect: print x.shape 

- ●Print shapes!! Make sure broadcasting is done properly. ●Print types and values. 

- ●Checking if two float arrays are approximately equal (element-wise) `○` **`np.allclose(x, y) # Can also specify tolerance`** 

- ●Checking if an array is close to zero (e.g. gradient) `○` **`np.allclose(x, 0) # Broadcasting`** 

- ●Selecting all elements less than 0 from an array `○` **`x[x < 0] # Returns 1-dim array`** 

81
topic: Unsure of what you can do with an object? Use type() and dir()!!

text:
## **Environment management** 

- ●Problem: 

   - ○Python 3 is not backward-compatible with Python 2 

   - ○Countless Python packages and their dependencies 

   - ○Different projects require different packages ■Even worse, different versions of the same package! 

## ●Solution: 

- ○Keep multiple Python environments that are isolated from each other 

- ○Each environment… 

   - ■can use different Python versions 

   - ■keeps its own set of packages 

■can be easily replicated (e.g. on a VM, friend’s laptop, etc.) 

82
topic: Unsure of what you can do with an object? Use type() and dir()!!

text:
## **Anaconda** 

- ●Anaconda is a popular Python environment/package manager 

   - ○Install from https://www.anaconda.com/download/ 

   - ○Supports Windows, Linux, macOS 

   - ○Basic workflow 

## **`$ source activate <environment_name>`** 

**`<... do stuff ...>`** 

## **`$ deactivate`** 

- ●Other environments won’t be affected by anything you do 

- ●Allows you to run a different version of Python for each environment 

83
topic: Unsure of what you can do with an object? Use type() and dir()!!

text:
## **Virtualenv** 

- ●Virtualenv is another popular Python environment manager ○Only specifies different packages per environment ○Doesn’t help run different Python version 

   - ○Installation from 

   - https://virtualenv.pypa.io/en/stable/installation/ 

   - ○Basic workflow 

      - **`$ mkdir <environment_directory>`** 

**`$ virtualenv <environment_directory>`** 

**`$ source <env_dir>/bin/activate`** 

**`$ pip install <package>`** 

84
